title: Erdős-Hajnal Conjecture
statement_text: For every graph $H$, there exists $c > 0$ such that every $H$-free graph $G$ satisfies $\max(\omega(G), \alpha(G)) \geq |V(G)|^c$.
context_text: Stated as Conjecture 1.1 and attributed to Erdős and Hajnal [6, 7], this conjecture has been proved only for very restricted graphs $H$; in particular it remains open when $H$ is a five-vertex path. The paper's title and results are directly situated within the programme of verifying special cases of this conjecture.
paper_contribution: Proves that for every caterpillar subdivision $H$ there exists $c > 0$ such that every graph $G$ that is both $H$-free and $\bar{H}$-free satisfies $\max(\omega(G), \alpha(G)) \geq |V(G)|^c$, extending results of Bousquet–Lagoutte–Thomassé (paths) and Choromanski–Falik–Liebenau–Patel–Pilipczuk (hooks).
notes: Referenced as [6, 7] in the paper; bibliography not reproduced in the extracted text so attributed_year is left null.

title: Conjecture 1.5 (sparse strong EH-property)
statement_text: A graph $H$ has the sparse strong EH-property if and only if $H$ is a forest.
context_text: The sparse strong EH-property of $H$ requires that there exists $\varepsilon > 0$ such that every $H$-free graph $G$ with $|V(G)| \geq 2$ either has a vertex with at least $\varepsilon|V(G)|$ neighbours or contains two anticomplete sets of vertices each of cardinality at least $\varepsilon|V(G)|$. Erdős's construction shows the property forces $H$ to be a forest; the conjecture asserts the converse.
notes: The paper explicitly notes that since submission both conjectures in 1.5 have been proved in reference [4].

title: Conjecture 1.5 (symmetric strong EH-property)
statement_text: A graph $H$ has the symmetric strong EH-property if and only if one of $H$, $\bar{H}$ is a forest.
context_text: The symmetric strong EH-property of $H$ requires that there exists $\varepsilon > 0$ such that every graph $G$ that is both $H$-free and $\bar{H}$-free with $|V(G)| \geq 2$ contains two disjoint sets of vertices each of cardinality at least $\varepsilon|V(G)|$ that are either complete or anticomplete to each other. The first conjecture of 1.5 implies the second via a theorem of Rödl [13]; both are described as reminiscent of the Gyárfás–Sumner conjecture.
notes: The paper explicitly notes that since submission both conjectures in 1.5 have been proved in reference [4].
